speaker: interviewee
text: I might mention it, on the tape, that my stories are many, and hanging--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I gave it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: As we go on a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: As we go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a little [? one. ?] [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three, an interview with Robert Ness on August the 24th, 1995. I just wanted to ask, before we go on, by this time-- November of 1941-- the Einsatzgruppen had been operating--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- had, had you or your family heard of any of the massacres?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. That's why we were going already outside of the ghetto, and hiding, and so on. Because we heard that in this town they killed 5,000, in this town they killed all the population, that suddenly, 2,000 people were taken. They were digging graves. 200 people, 12,000 people-- the men-- were taken, digging graves. I mean, the, the city was full of rumors. Suddenly, there's a lot of Lithuanians in town, and there are a lot of others. I think Lithuanians were on the [INAUDIBLE] that time. And [INAUDIBLE] is full. So it was-- oh, absolutely. That's why it's already-- that's why father went the one side, my sisters went in another. And my mother and the aunts stayed in the same place. But just to finish up the story with [? stamps ?] there. So finally, Ranger-- but the point was, when he came in, this young soldier-- because it's so unusual, that's why I think it's worthwhile mentioning it-- those other two soldiers told me to sit down behind the cabinet. So when somebody goes in, they couldn't see me immediately. They would see just a table. I was sitting behind that closet. And then they told me that Ranger is in. And he came in, and I started-- and he said to me-- and I also saw a lieutenant there. And the lieutenant, two [? gendarmes ?] came and they wanted for [? many ?] people to, for this, I presume for the slaughter. But I did hear enough to hear him say [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] For those kind of things, I do not have men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not have men to contribute to, to-- for these kind of things. It was interesting. But anyway, so Ranger comes in and he start asking what I doing here. I said, well, you know, I came-- and I say-- what-- where are your par-- I said [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't know [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you a Jew? Yes. I said, yes. He said, where are your parents? I said, I don't know. I said, my mother, I don't know. And so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me to his place where he lived, and we came back. And then he says to me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Hide yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- it was like a block. It wasn't such a hard word. I didn't understand it. And he could realize suddenly that I don't understand it, That I don't-- it doesn't register. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he moved me on the ground floor, opened the door, and pushed me into a, in a room. And I was there. And suddenly I hear noises in the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the washroom, I kind of went to the washroom. And lo and behold, there was another Jewish boy there. His aunt and uncle were working for the Germans in this particular school. This, this, It was under Commander [? Nein, ?] Special Unit [? Nein. ?] And what they used to collect, the dead bodies of the German soldiers that were killed in the-- in around [INAUDIBLE] and give them a burial.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he thought that his aunt and uncle were living, working for the Germans. It's a safer place. So he came to stay overnight with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it just so happened the school, big building, the whole operation started from this place. They were drinking and so on. There were a few thousand gendarmes and the other forces. That's where they started.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they started, they had the-- they got the French. The first fish they caught was his uncles and aunt. And there we were. He somehow or rather stayed in the washroom or whatever. Be that as it may, he jumped out of the window. He was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came around 5 o'clock, that Ranger came back. He said the lieutenant wants to see us. So we came out. The two of us now are going. And he-- and I kind of felt like it's-- you know, I'd already heard the discussion on that stairs, so I figured he is not that bad. So he says, it's finished and you can go home. It's all over. Then I kind of said, OK. So we started to go through the front. And he said that the other ones are coming back, and you cannot stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to go through the front, like I came in. He said, no, no, no. Somebody, that Ranger told me down the stairs, through the back door. And we went across the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other boy said his grandmother lives not far from here. And there was a, a, a pick up truck, a big one. A guard was sleeping. We kind of passed by quietly around him. And we were in his grandmother's place. Nobody was there, so we-- they were in the yard, in the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews were killed that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 10,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not on that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were in hiding outside of the ghetto. They got there. [? Also ?] the woman was afraid to hold them, so they started to look. And they wound up in the woods where the killings were taking place. But they managed to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one of your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother and aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed in that place, so they were killed that day. November 14th. My mother and Aunt Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- when did you find out about their deaths?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out as we came back. Father came back, and he came, and he said-- he started to cry and saying-- I remember the words-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It's dark in my corners. And that's it. We knew that because-- first of all, some people came back from the graves, that managed to throw off the dead bodies and came back. So we knew that the killings was taking place. And that's where we-- and we didn't know it was a [INAUDIBLE] life or not. But after two weeks later, they finally showed up and told us the story, whatever they had to go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then life continued in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life continued again. I was still friendly with Ranger. He was in town for a long time. He came to our-- to the place in our ghetto, came over, visited us, brought some food. They used to get stuff. We used to give him stamps. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your father support himself during this time? With the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I suppose he must have had money from the liquidation of the stores that we had. He must have had some money. I don't think Father worked. If he worked, it was just pro forma, just for the-- just as though he worked, you know, but he--

speaker: interviewer
text: But all four of you were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: All four of us. Now are only four, Pola, [? Lisa, ?] my father, and I. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find or see your mother's grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was big graves. Who know it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a mass grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mass graves. There were four or five supposedly, after the war. They've looked-- no. They were killed on that day. It was the 14th of November. On the Jewish calendar, it was 24 days in Marcheshvan, the months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came--

speaker: interviewer
text: June came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: June 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: --then we moved in different places in the ghetto until the ghetto wound up being smaller and smaller. And , June 1942 comes around. And suddenly, the ghetto is closed and this. By that time, it was already-- there was wires and so on. But by that time, people knew that killings are taking place. So everybody and his brother was just building the place to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you were-- you entered in a corridor. There was a little yard inside, between two houses. Somehow or other, under the house, under the yard, was a hole and people were able to walk in there and so forth. Did anyone have any weapons that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. So what happened was when they started to liquidate that particular ghetto, they found nobody, just about nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a section of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Section. And now I'm talking now June 29, 1942. So they started to burn down the ghetto. So the wooden houses were burning, and people were starting to go out because of the smoke, and the burning, and the [? fire. ?] But in our houses, we were in brick houses. There were about four or six brick houses in the main street, and we were among one of them. And we were hiding. We didn't go-- my elder sister, Pola, had a friend. And he came, and he said they had a good hiding place. And she went with him. And that's the last time we saw Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever learn how she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Supposedly, she was trying to run away through the wires and was killed around on the wires of the ghetto, but we never-- it's just-- didn't have any specifics on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you, and your sister, and your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's only-- it's my father, [? Lisa, ?] and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were together in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- yes. But the way we hid, there was an oven. But you know, the white Russian oven, behind the ovens you have like a sleeping room, or holding wood, or so forth. So we just went behind it and had a little curtain, which is nothing. And we were just the three of us. And we heard the gendarmes going in, and the polices going in, up and down. And quiet. Then at night time, we moved out, a hiding place that we had above a wood shack. And that's where my father, [? Lisa, ?] and I went there. And there was still a one old Jew from [? Margolies ?]. And he was praying. And he wanted the water for his nails. And we wanted-- it was-- but he just didn't give a-- didn't care. So we finally kept it down. Just let him use the water. That's it. He wanted the nail, for the nails for praying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto- the entire ghetto was liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, it was-- well, suddenly, they still didn't get everybody out. Then Aaron came, my [? Lisa's ?] friend. And he was working in a German beutelager. Beutelager means booty camp, war booty camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was working there, and his German. people that he worked for, they came to the ghetto to take him out. So they took him out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he managed, in a few days later, to take out [? Lisa. ?] She was dressed up as a man. And he took her out with his German guard, going, and they went out of the ghetto. Then the story said that suddenly, they took-- everybody's getting a yellow card. That-- and you have to go to prison and get a yellow card. And that's it. Then you are free. And that's when-- I'm talking June. Now I'm talking about the beginning of July, 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to jail. We went to the city prison. And suddenly, they didn't let out anybody. And before you know it, they had about a few thousand people collected there, and nobody's going out. In the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the prison. In different chambers, common-- you know, this chamber, that room, and so forth. I remember there was a girl that was lying next to my father. And suddenly, the guards-- I think it was Ukrainians-- called her, took her away. I didn't know anything about rape, or I had never heard. I didn't know what it's all about. I never heard it. And she was taken out. They must have been, they must have been raped. And then she came back in tears, and lay down there. And then suddenly, we came back into the big yard, and the police was there and with, with big sticks. And hitting-- I remember that the sticks were broken the length from hitting over the head of people. And everybody, out of your pockets what you have, and everything else. And they took out about 15, 20 people. And then the rest, they pushed back into the big-- into the small, little square. And that was it. They were already starting to take out people on the, the big trucks to the graves. But they left out some of them. So suddenly, the German head of the police, that Hauptmann, Banke, he walked in there, and a fellow comes in from [? Ausland ?], it's a German firm with that brown uniform. It's [INAUDIBLE]. And he starts reading people of his workers, so-called workers. My father was so-called ropeman. He was a ropeman as you are a ropeman. But anyone who was a roper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- a roper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rope. R O P E. Making ropes, OK? Supposedly he worked as a rope man. And suddenly, he reads out the names. He has hard time with the Polish names, so he skips them. And he goes through the other one. Nussbaum. And my father was taken out from one side to the other, but I am still on the other side. And the distance from my father, it was like from here to the corner. That's as far as I was away from him. And I started to address that [? Hauptmann. ?] I say, Hauptmann, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And I've been kept, and I want to say something to you. I want to tell you something. And I wanted to tell him that I am the son of my father, and he's here. And then the gendarme was standing next to him. There were a flock of gendarmes standing there, next to-- and father was telling, and it's my son, my son, and in tears. And suddenly, the gendarme, while I was still talking, [? Hauptmann ?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And then he just winked with his finger to me, to move over to the side. And I was right there at my father's in a matter of minutes. And then I got the yellow card. This [? Hauptmann ?] came up. I remember it like today. When he came up, he had my card. It was number 1,000. All it was was a little bar. A card, three by five. A yellow card with the seal of the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE]. And the number was in just 1,000, didn't have a name, nothing. And my number was 1,000. And [? Banke ?] came up-- that was that captain I was telling you about. And he said to me, here, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Number 1,000. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You'll have luck. Number 1,000, you'll live long. What I inside felt, it's a different story. I figured, most probably, it's going to be another something. But anyway, be that as it may, I got the card. And we were the last 50 people out-- not even 50, maybe 15, 20-- out from this little square, to the big square, and out of the, out of the prison. [CLAPPING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were free men again. Free men? In other words, you had survived one of those other slaughters, and kill, murders. And-- How long then did  you remain at--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was July. Oh, I think August. Just about a month. There was still-- as a matter of fact I was a witness in that case. Banke. Banke died, but they had a trial--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: The captain. He died, but he was included as a defendant. And there were the two gendarmes were there, and also Erren, E-R-R-E-N. He was the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] of the whole area. And I had an encounter with him too. When-- after that story, I was moved to another part of the ghetto, next to the bridge. My father was working, and I started to go outside of the ghetto. I was always running around without the yellow shines, nothing. Looked like a Polish boy, you know, a gentile boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went-- I used to buy cigarettes, [? bring, ?] and eggs, and other stuff. And one day there was a-- not a liquidation, but a, a search. And they arrest-- they took about 25 boys, 25 or 50 children, 30, 40 children. And we were right square on the banks of the river Shchara. That was the river going through Slonim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to-- I heard somebody calling him, calling this Erren, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So I started to address him, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I started my father was working for [? Ausland, ?] and there was some other children that are also working, that our parents, their fathers, are working for outside. And we don't have anything. And believe it or not, it's about eight or 10, me among them, and we went back into the ghetto. The others, they took away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably got killed, or they didn't send them to a sanitarium.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come then to Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- it must have been next month, in August. Father must have made some arrangements to be able to run away. I don't know what arrangement was-- I remember that [? Lisa ?] came. She was in a different camp. She was in this beutelager where Aaron was working. I remember she came. And we got out of the ghetto. And suddenly, we were out of the ghetto. And somebody met us with a, with a horse and buggy, a farmer, a Polish farmer, I think it was. And he took us through the border. Keep in mind that by that time, Grodno was part of Germany. Białystok was part of Germany, not occupied territories. And, and then we heard that it's in the German Reich the Jews are being treated better than what is in the occupied territories. And then we came by way of Volkovysk. And we got to Grodno. But it was also quite-- we had to go through. It's walking, and driving, and so on. And we came to Grodno in-- must have been September, October of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you entered the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Entered the ghetto. There were two ghettos in Grodno at the time, number one and two. We entered through the main ghetto, number one. And number two was outside of-- around the suburb, in a different location. And we there. [DRINKS] Then they started to liquidate. They liquidated the ghetto number two. And then they started to bring in Jews from-- up from the smaller towns around Grodno. I remember [? Kalbasine ?] was one of them that came in. There was some other small little towns, and they came into Grodno. And then they started to liquidate that ghetto. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the beginning, was with lists, but we were hiding. We already had experience of Slonim. We didn't take any word from them or promises about going to camps or working in the east. We knew that it was all just lies and it didn't have any foundation. And so we were hiding in different places, in a number of times. And until Aaron-- then Aaron came later on. Aaron came much later. Aaron is [? Lisa's-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Lisa's ?] friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friend and now husband. And he was working outside, at the Gestapo, and building construction there. And he and a friend met a certain Polish fellow who supposedly was a railroad guy, and supposedly would help to try to get away. Vilna was quiet at the time, suddenly.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Vilna ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vilna ghetto. After it was slaughtered and killings in '41 and '42. You know, they kill about 90,000 people around Ponary, a suburb of Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were thinking in terms of running away. So-- and suddenly the ghetto was closed. So somebody had to make arrangement to go and find Tadek I was-- Tadek was the name of the-- Tadek Tadek was the Polish fellow. Soroka, S-O-R-O-K-A. Tadek Soroka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soroka. [? Tadeos ?] Soroka. He was later-- after the war, we took him to Israel. He was honored as one as the righteous. And he was here in the United States too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was-- he was a railway man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Supposedly. We find out later, that he had phony papers. But that was our railway man. And so I was going out to make contact. And I went to-- I got the address. I found it. I never was in Grodno, outside. It's the first time outside of the ghetto. The first time I am in Grodno, period. And so I go. I found the place. And he met me. And he said-- I told him, the ghetto is closed. But he says, OK, to meet him on-- I think it was Thursday or Wednesday. Thursday, I think-- to meet at 5 o'clock in a public place. And he will take us-- take the group to Vilna on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, [? Lisa-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. There was no discussion of who. I mean, we were talking about-- he would take at most three to five people, or something like that. So it would have been [? Lisa, ?] Aaron, Daddy, and I, right there, four.

speaker: interviewer
text: For money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether it was money. Most probably. I think at the time, money must have been discussed too. But I know I gave him nothing. I don't know whether my father gave him anything. At that time, he was already on a friendly basis that he would've done money or no money because he already took a group before him two, twice. He took two people, and two people, four people--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Grodno to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Grodno to Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you briefly summarize the story of how you got from Grodno to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there is one story that I've got to tell, because this is another hanging between life and death. Really hanging between life and death. And I testified to it in the court afterward. [? Visa ?] was the head of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go outside the ghetto. They push me through the window. There was a church. I go through, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLAPS] I'm outside. And then I go to, to Tadek. And lo and behold, yes. Arrangements were made. Mind my own business. So now I have to give the message the [? Lisa ?] and Aaron. One half of the ghetto was on one side of the street, was the Jewish ghetto. And it was already a small one, on some [? covered ?] street. On the other side, outside of the ghetto, was-- the other side of the street was the Aryan side, so to speak. As luck would have it, the corridor, in the corridor between two houses were bombed out during the war. So there was nobody living there. And that corridor was facing smack to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Lisa and Aaron and there-- and my father was standing on one side. And I was on the outside. They were shouting in Yiddish. I was shouting in Polish. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLAPS] Pass the message. And everything's fine, and then-- [CLAPS] And off I went. It was Tuesday. It was also lucky. It was Tuesday. It was a market day, so was a lot of farmers in the city there. And it was full, so I got lost in the, in the crowd. I went to a postage store, bought some stamps. I bought a cross, and I bought a prayer book, a Catholic prayer book in Polish. And I was just killing time, to just stay and now to go to sleep maybe later on. And as I walk, just killing time, suddenly a little girl goes up to me. And she says-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you are from the ghetto. I said, I, I don't know-- what are you talking about? I don't know what you're talking about. What ghetto? Who? What? And she said, don't be afraid. I, myself, from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CLAPS] And I was in trouble, obviously. She was outside of the ghetto. She was eight years old, maybe nine. And somebody recognized me. Now I have a companion. And within minutes, a flock of children screaming-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Jews, Jews, Jews. And a, and a German gendarme runs up with a revolver in his hand. Halt, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And I start my song, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Halt! And, And I said, I, I was just asking-- she was asking the street. Or I was-- I, I, I just-- I am from a village 30 kilometers away. I came to the marketplace. I brought a cross, like this. I bought some stamps. I show him-- give him the schpiel. And he just about wasn't quite convinced. He says, well, to the ghetto [? Vaha ?]. He's taking me to the ghetto [? Vaha. ?] This with a Germans gendarmes, which were watching. It's a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep in mind, this is now Germany. It's no more you have, you have Lithuanians, and Latvians. And in other words, it's Germany proper, so to speak. And he brings me to the ghetto [? Vaha. ?] And they bring me in. And I say [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no, I don't know German. Just a little bit, what I learned in school. [? Fenster ?], table, you know, window, table, door. Just-- And he tells them the story. So this little one, she says that she has an aunt. And that's where she lives. And somebody takes her to there. And of course, this whole story fell apart. [CLAPS] And they throw her into the ghetto. But when she was thrown into the ghetto, suddenly my father got wind, and [? Lisa ?] got wind, and Aaron, that I was arrested, that I'm in the ghetto [? Vaha. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were trying, kind of, to get-- it didn't get to anything. So they approached one gendarme there, and he kind of-- when I was in the washroom-- said, don't tell them that you're a Jew. You're doing fine. I said, what are you-- I didn't know that he-- I said, I don't know what you're talking about. I'm not a Jew. I'm not Jewish. And so some of them-- that's it? No, we just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two minutes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we can wind it up. We'll wind up this story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Let me wind up this particular story. So this guy asked me to take off my shoe, because people who were running away were wearing a few shirts, two pair, three pair, four shirts. I only had one shirt, but I did have two pair of socks, and two pair of underwear. So it's cold in Poland. So that kind of pairs. I had a little other problem on my hand. My father gave me five-ruble gold piece before leaving, just in case of travel, I will need it. And when he was pushing, all of the sudden, I didn't remember where I put the five ruble. I moved it away from my pocket, and I put it into my boots. I had boots on. And I remember, that the policeman was on my right side, so I must have done it on the left side. So I took off the right shoe. And sure enough, the five-ruble piece was not there. So it was just the socks and so forth. And then-- so, that passed. But then it became evening already. So that I should stay over because I cannot go on. It's curfew time. And so one of them comes in and he says, put down one and three zeros. So we'll see whether he's Jewish or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fellow puts on one. I put on, I say eins. He puts on a zero. I say, zehn. He puts on another zero. I say, hundert. He puts on the third zero. I say tysiac. Tysiac is the Polish for 1,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, Deutsch! I said, I don't know in Deutsch. And he says that-- then he says [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I'm making it short-- so he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. If he would be Jew, he would say--
